item.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20245 Exploitation, codfish GitHub Action Compromise, and Edgecution Malware
item.content:encoded:
Mandiant reveals how Cisco SD-WAN zero-day attacks gained root access — Must Read

Mandiant says attackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day CVE-2026-20245 after gaining device access, using a crafted tenant-upload CSV to create a rogue root account and take full control. The attackers also used anti-forensic cleanup and may have relied on rogue peering or previously stolen certificates, so organizations should patch and check SD-WAN devices for unauthorized peering and compromise indicators.

  • Mandiant reported that hackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerability CVE-2026-20245 in zero-day attacks to create rogue root accounts on targeted devices.
  • CVE-2026-20245 is a high-severity command injection flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, Controller, and Validator that allows authenticated attackers with local access to execute arbitrary commands as root by uploading a crafted file.
  • Mandiant said the attackers used a tenant-upload feature in the SD-WAN command-line interface to upload a malicious CSV file named "evil_tenant.csv" that created a new root-level account named "troot."
  • The attackers changed the default admin account password, extracted configuration information for edge devices, controllers, and SD-WAN templates, and later restored the original password to reduce detection.
  • Mandiant observed anti-forensic activity including restoring modified configuration files, deleting the malicious CSV payload and temporary files, erasing evidence of the rogue root account, and running a validation script to confirm cleanup.

Compromised GitHub action codfish/semantic-release-action steals CI/CD secrets — Must Read

The codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action was compromised after attackers force-pushed malicious commits and repointed multiple version tags, causing affected CI workflows to run obfuscated code that could steal release secrets such as GITHUB_TOKEN and NPM_TOKEN. Users should stop using the affected tags, check for the listed malicious commits and payload hash, and rotate any exposed credentials.

  • On Jun 24, 2026, the codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action was compromised when an attacker force-pushed two malicious commits and repointed sixteen tags, including v2, v3, v4, and v5.
  • Any workflow referencing the affected tags will pull and run the attacker’s code on its next CI run, and these workflows often contain GITHUB_TOKEN and sometimes NPM_TOKEN credentials with publish access.
  • The malicious commits changed action.yml from a Docker-based action into a composite action that first ran a clean pinned version of the original action and then installed Bun and executed an obfuscated index.js payload.
  • The injected index.js payload was 781,580 bytes, had SHA-256 hash 9f93d77d32833a515bc406c46da477142bb1ac2babeecb6aa42f98669a6db015, and contained the dead drop marker string thebeautifulsnadsoftime.
  • The article links the payload to the Miasma credential stealing toolkit, which uses GitHub’s public commit search API to find marker strings and deliver commands through matching commits.

Malicious Edge extension abuses Native Messaging as bridge to malware — Should Read

A malicious Microsoft Edge extension called Edgecution is being used in ransomware-linked attacks to abuse Chrome Native Messaging, escape the browser sandbox, and launch a Python backdoor on victim systems. The campaign starts with fake Microsoft update pages promoted through Teams impersonation, highlighting the need to tightly monitor browser extensions and native messaging host configurations.

  • A malicious Microsoft Edge extension dubbed Edgecution has been used in a ransomware attack to escape the browser sandbox and deploy a Python-based backdoor.
  • The attack begins with an attacker posing as IT support personnel on Microsoft Teams and directing employees to a fraudulent page under the pretense of installing a spam filter update.
  • Zscaler researchers believe Edgecution is deployed by an initial access broker connected to the Payouts Kings ransomware operation.
  • The malware uses Chrome Native Messaging to let the Edge extension communicate with a local Python-based backdoor that can execute shell commands, run PowerShell, run Python code, write files, enumerate processes, and gather system information.
  • Zscaler recommends that organizations strengthen monitoring of browser extensions and enforce strict controls over native messaging host configurations.

Three ‘cybercrime as a service’ operations undercut by Microsoft, law enforcement — Should Read

Microsoft and international law enforcement disrupted cybercrime-as-a-service infrastructure for SocGholish, Amadey and StealC, seizing hundreds of servers/domains, recovering stolen credentials and identifying €41 million in criminal crypto assets. The operation targeted the malware supply chain used to enable ransomware, fraud and broader intrusions.

  • Europol and Microsoft announced that an international two-week operation dismantled 326 servers and 142 domains used by cybercrime-as-a-service operations.
  • The operation targeted infrastructure used to distribute the SocGholish, Amadey and StealC malware families, which other criminals paid to use for further crimes.
  • Investigators recovered about 27 million stolen login credentials and identified crypto assets of criminal origin valued at €41 million ($47 million).
  • Europol said SocGholish was found on 14,971 infected websites belonging to everyday retailers and is tied to the Russian cybercrime gang Evil Corp.
  • Microsoft said disrupting Amadey and StealC together should have an exponential effect because Amadey is mainly used to break in while StealC steals passwords and other sensitive data.

Trust No One: Automating macOS Privilege Escalation at Scale — Should Read

Researchers describe a macOS privilege-escalation technique that lets a standard user impersonate trusted app components through CDHash cache and NIB payload abuse to call privileged XPC services, potentially disabling EDR and MDM tools without admin rights. Vendors should harden XPC client validation using active code-signing checks rather than relying on cached trust.

  • The article describes a macOS privilege escalation technique that allows standard user accounts to silently disable enterprise security products without administrator credentials, kernel exploits, or triggering security alerts.
  • The technique chains CDHash kernel cache exploitation with NIB payload injection to impersonate trusted application components and invoke privileged XPC methods without authentication.
  • The research was validated against CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor, where full sensor unloading was achieved from a standard user account, and against Kandji MDM Agent, where permanent agent deactivation was achieved via a two-phase XPC chain.
  • CrowdStrike has added detections and preventions for all supported versions of the macOS Falcon sensor, and the Kandji issue has been fixed with CVE-2026-39118 assigned.
  • The author built an open-source automated framework called XPC Hunter to discover exploitable XPC privilege escalation surfaces across installed macOS applications at scale.

DraftKings hacker 'Snoopy' sentenced to 18 months in prison — May Read

Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old hacker known as “Snoopy,” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for helping compromise tens of thousands of DraftKings accounts in a 2022 credential-stuffing scheme that stole about $600,000. He was also ordered to pay forfeiture and restitution totaling nearly $1.8 million.

  • Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old from Minnesota who used the alias “Snoopy,” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for his role in hacking DraftKings accounts in the November 2022 cyberattack.
  • Austad pleaded guilty in December 2025 to conspiracy to commit computer intrusion and admitted that he and co-conspirators compromised 60,000 DraftKings user accounts.
  • During the attack, the hackers added payment methods under their control to 1,600 accounts and stole $600,000.
  • The U.S. Department of Justice said Austad operated a shop named after Snoopy where he sold access to stolen accounts, and his cryptocurrency accounts received approximately $465,000 in assets.
  • In addition to prison, Austad received three years of supervised release and was ordered to pay $463,684 in forfeiture and $1,327,061 in restitution.

Why Is an AI Image Company Building a Body Scanner? — May Read

Midjourney plans to roll out a consumer full-body ultrasound scanner in spas without FDA clearance, raising major medical-device security, data volume, and regulatory concerns. Health-ISAC warns that spa users may not receive HIPAA protections and that cybersecurity must be addressed early in development.

  • Midjourney has announced its first hardware product, a full-body ultrasound scanner that it plans to deploy in consumer spas.
  • The article states that Midjourney plans a consumer rollout of the scanner with no FDA clearance yet.
  • Health-ISAC’s Phil Englert raised potential data security concerns, noting that a single full-body ultrasound study can produce roughly 800 terabytes of data.
  • Englert said every ultrasound element that emits and receives sound adds to the attack surface, and a threat model would need to consider whether transducers could be manipulated during a scan.
  • Englert warned that a spa charging customers directly without insurance may fall outside HIPAA, so users should understand whether they are customers or patients and what protections apply.

How to evaluate Cloud Detection & Response (CDR) platforms and vendors — Optional

The article provides a checklist for evaluating Cloud Detection and Response platforms, emphasizing that buyers should prioritize broad visibility across cloud, SaaS, identity, workloads, and AI systems over feature counts. The key test is whether a CDR platform can measurably reduce detection time, response time, and blast radius in real-world cloud attacks.

  • The article says Cloud Detection and Response platforms should be evaluated by real operational needs rather than by feature lists alone.
  • A CDR platform should provide visibility across cloud control planes, SaaS, identity providers, workloads, storage, databases, APIs, networking logs, and configuration changes.
  • The article argues that identity telemetry must be central because attackers in cloud environments can use valid credentials and chain access across SaaS, cloud, and workloads.
  • The checklist says CDR platforms should use AI-driven triage, cross-domain correlation, automatic attack path reconstruction, and machine-speed detection to reduce analyst noise.
  • The article says the final decision should be based on whether a CDR platform measurably reduces time-to-detect, time-to-respond, and blast radius across critical cloud, SaaS, and identity paths.

item.title: codfish GitHub Action Compromise, Operation Endgame, and Exploited Ubiquiti CVE-2026-34908–34910
Bajaj ransomware, Cordyceps GitHub flaws, Mistic and SharkLoader campaigns

Federal PQC deadlines, Scattered Spider plea, and AI agent supply-chain risks
